content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 29, 2016

Betty Ann Downing
Olson Hagel & Fishburn, LLP
3605 Long Beach Blvd., Suite 426
Long Beach, CA 90807-6010

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-177

Dear Ms. Downing:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Assemblymember Patrick
O'Donnell and Los Angeles: Orange County Democrats United regarding the permissible use of
campaign funds provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

In regards to a state candidate controlled election committee and a general purpose recipient
committee, may either committee expend campaign funds to pay for a reception and celebration of
life event honoring a long-time California Democratic Party ("Party") political activist, held after a
Party Executive Board meeting and attended by Party officials, donors and activists?

CONCLUSIONS

Yes. The event was directly related to the political purpose of each committee and each may
expend campaign funds for the event as "gifts" to the attendees, so long as the gifts have a total
cumulative value of less than $250 to each individual under Section 89513(f)(3).

FACTS

On June 19, 2016 a reception and a celebration of life service ("Event") was held in
recognition of Eric Bradley, a highly-respected Party activist who had passed away unexpectedly on
May 15, 2016. The Event was scheduled immediately after a Party Executive Board meeting, and
attendees included those who attended the Party Executive Board meeting, elected officials,
political donors, and activists involved with the Los Angeles: Orange County Democrats United
("Democrats United") committee. The Event was unrelated to the private memorial service held by
Eric Bradley's family one month prior.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-177
Page No. 2

According to your facts, Eric Bradley was a political activist and highly respected within the
Democratic Party, the Long Beach and Southern California political communities. He most recently
served as the Assistant Treasurer to the Democrats United committee. The Democrats United
committee is a general purpose recipient committee, with a mission to "support or oppose state and
local candidates." Eric Bradley had been involved in this organization since its inception. Eric
Bradley was also a long-time supporter of Assemblymember Patrick O'Donnell. He volunteered on
each of Assemblymember O'Donnell's campaigns and served as an advisor to him as an elected
official.

The primary focus of the celebration of life service was to "honor Eric Bradley's political
efforts" and encourage "attendees to continue his political legacy by volunteering on campaigns, to
register voters, and to engage in political activities." Formal remarks were made by Congressman
Allan Lowenthal, Assemblymember Patrick O'Donnell, former Assemblymember Hector de la
Torre, former Long Beach Mayor Bob Foster, and Vice Chair of the California Democratic
Party/Chair of the Los Angeles County Democratic Party Eric Bauman. In addition, many current
and former elected officials, political activists and others provided remarks during the service.

The costs of the Event included food, beverages, site rental (including audio visual
equipment, chairs/tables/podium set up, etc.) and decorations. No one was paid a fee to
participate. Everyone was a volunteer, except for the staff provided by the event site who handled
beverage service, set up and take down services, and similar activities. These costs were either paid
by friends of Eric Bradley or are still owed to the venue. His family has not been asked, and there is
no expectation that they would pay anything toward the Event.

ANALYSIS

The Act's provisions restricting the "personal use" of campaign funds are designed to
prevent candidates, elected officials, and others who control the expenditures of campaign funds
from benefiting directly from their campaign activities. The general rule is that an expenditure of
campaign funds must be reasonably related to a political, legislative or governmental purpose.
A higher standard, requiring a "direct relationship" applies where an expenditure of campaign funds
confers a substantial personal benefit on those with authority to approve the expenditure of
campaign funds; or if the expenditure is for a particular class of activity.

With regard to the use of campaign funds, Section 89510 provides that "[a]ll contributions
deposited into the campaign account shall be deemed to be held in trust for expenses associated
with the election of the candidate or for expenses associated with holding office." Section 89512
states:

An expenditure to seek office is within the lawful execution of the trust imposed
by Section 89510 if it is reasonably related to a political purpose. An expenditure
associated with holding office is within the lawful execution of the trust imposed
by Section 89510 if it is reasonably related to a legislative or governmental
purpose. Expenditures which confer a substantial personal benefit shall be directly
related to a political, legislative or governmental purpose.

File No. A-16-177
Page No. 3

Section 89511(b)(3) defines "substantial personal benefit" as an expenditure of campaign funds which results in a direct personal benefit with a value of more than $200 to a candidate, elected officer, or any individual or individuals with authority to approve the expenditure of campaign funds held by a committee. Section 89512.5 contains similar provisions applicable to non-candidate controlled committees, such as a general purpose committee.

The particular class of activities requiring the higher, "directly related," standard include: travel, professional services, specialty clothing, fines, tickets for sporting or entertainment events, or personal gifts. (Section 89513.) A gift is defined as any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received. (Section 82028.) Section 89513(f)(1) permits the use of campaign funds for gifts only if there is a direct relationship between the expenditure for the gifts and a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.

Here, each committee would be making a gift to the attendees of the Event, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not provided. Assemblymember O'Donnell's election committee subject to the requirements of Section 89510. Democrats United as a general purpose committee is subject to the requirements of Section 89512.5. Each committee's funds in accordance with the standard that these gift expenditures be directly related, under these facts, to a political purpose of the committee.

Candidate Controlled Election Committee: O'Donnell for Assembly 2016

An expenditure of campaign funds is directly related to a political purpose of a candidate controlled committee when the payment is necessary to the quest for elective office and is incurred in connection with activities designed to enhance the candidate's visibility in the community and to inform voters of the candidate's positions on issues relevant to his or her campaign for elective office. (Tucker Advice Letter, No. A-91-418.)

For Assemblymember O'Donnell's election committee, in addition to being a tribute to the party activist the Event enhances the Assemblymember's visibility in the community, especially the Party community of key donors and political activists. Such political community support is an essential part of seeking elective office. The expenditures are permissible so long as the gift to each attendee does not exceed the limitation of $250 total cumulative value to attendees in a calendar year. (Section 89513(f)(3).)

This Event is also similar to two types of "recognition" expenditures deemed directly related to a political purpose of a candidate controlled committee: an election night "thank you" event for campaign workers and a gift (less than $250 in cumulative annual value) to a retiring Senate employee. (See Demers Advice Letter, No. A-92-586 and Murphy Advice Letter, No. A-92-116 on campaign staff events; see Bauer Advice Letter, A-08-045, candidate gift to staff; and Section 89513(f)(3).)

Additionally, the facts in this matter are distinguished from those presented in the Abramson Advice Letter A-09-246 (the use of candidate's controlled committee campaign funds for the candidate's own memorial service did not meet the "directly related" standard, in that it was

File No. A-16-177
Page No. 4

unrelated to the committee's primary objective to re-elect the candidate). This Event was a
recognition event in memoriam for a staff member and campaign activist, and not for the candidate.

General Purpose Recipient Committee: Los Angeles; Orange County Democrats United

In regards to a general purpose committee, in Kaufman Advice Letter, No. A-96-175, we
determined the following activities were directly related to the political, legislative or governmental
purpose of the committee:

Activities such as participation in political events and rallies, coordinating
political action aimed at influencing contract negotiations or budget decisions,
voter registration activities, providing political education to both union and
nonunion members, tracking legislation, initiatives and administrative decisions,
lobbying state and local officials, recruiting campaign volunteers, voter
registration activities, get-out-the-vote activities, phone banks to influence
elections or governmental activities, and administrative costs and tasks for all of
these activities appear to be directly related to political, legislative or
governmental purposes. Committee funds may be used to pay the costs of these
activities, including staff salaries.

For the Democrats United committee, whose mission is to support and oppose area
candidates, the Event served as "a political event." In addition to the purpose of recognizing past
efforts of a staff member and campaign activist, the Event was a gathering of those Party officials,
donors and activists who are important to campaign activities in the area. The expenditures are
permissible so long as the gift to each attendee has a total cumulative value of less than $250 in a
calendar year. (Section 89513(f)(3).)

Please note that individual attendees may be subject to disclosure and gift limit ($460)
requirements under the Act for the receipt of a gift from either committee. (Section 87200, et al.,
and Section 89503.)

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH: jgl

sections.analysis:
The Act's provisions restricting the "personal use" of campaign funds are designed to prevent candidates, elected officials, and others who control the expenditures of campaign funds from benefiting privately from their campaign activities. The general rule is that an expenditure of campaign funds must be reasonably related to a political, legislative or governmental purpose. A higher standard, requiring a "direct relationship" applies where an expenditure of campaign funds confers a substantial personal benefit on those with authority to approve the expenditure of campaign funds; or if the expenditure is for a particular class of activity.

With regard to the use of campaign funds, Section 89510 provides that "[a]ll contributions deposited into the campaign account shall be deemed to be held in trust for expenses associated with the election of the candidate or for expenses associated with holding office." Section 89512 states:

An expenditure associated with holding office is within the lawful execution of the trust imposed by Section 89510 if it is reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose. Expenditures which confer a substantial personal benefit shall be directly related to a political, legislative or governmental purpose.

Section 89511(b)(3) defines “substantial personal benefit” as an expenditure of campaign funds which results in a direct personal benefit with a value of more than $200 to a candidate, elected officer, or any individual or individuals with authority to approve the expenditure of campaign funds held by a committee. (Section 89513.) A gift is defined as any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received. (Section 82028.) Here, each committee would be making a gift to the attendees of the Event, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not provided. Assemblymember O’Donnell’s election committee is subject to the requirements of Section 89510. Democrats United as a general purpose committee is subject to the requirements of Section 89512.5. Each committee’s funds must be used in accordance with the standard that these gift expenditures be directly related, under these facts, to a political purpose of the committee.

Candidate Controlled Election Committee: O’Donnell for Assembly 2016

An expenditure of campaign funds is directly related to a political purpose of a candidate controlled committee when the payment is necessary to the quest for elective office and is incurred in connection with activities designed to enhance the candidate’s visibility in the community and to inform voters of the candidate’s positions on issues relevant to his or her campaign for elective office. This Event is also similar to two types of “recognition” expenditures deemed directly related to a political purpose of a candidate controlled committee: an election night “thank you” event for campaign workers and a gift (less than $250 in cumulative annual value) to a retiring Senate employee. (See Demers Advice Letter, No. A-92-586 and Murphy Advice Letter, No. A-92-116 on campaign staff events; see Bauer Advice Letter, A-08-045, candidate gift to staff; and Section 89513(f)(3).)

Additionally, the facts in this matter are distinguished from those presented in the Abramson Advice Letter A-09-246 (the use of candidate’s controlled committee campaign funds for the candidate’s own memorial service did not meet the “directly related” standard, in that it was

unrelated to the committee’s primary objective to re-elect the candidate). This Event was a recognition event *in memoriam* for a staff member and campaign activist, and not for the candidate.

**General Purpose Recipient Committee: Los Angeles: Orange County Democrats United**

In regards to a general purpose committee, in *Kaufman Advice Letter*, No. A-96-175, we determined the following activities were *directly* related to the political, legislative or governmental purpose of the committee:

Activities such as participation in political events and rallies, coordinating political action aimed at influencing contract negotiations or budget decisions, voter registration activities, providing political education to both union and nonunion members, tracking legislation, initiatives and administrative decisions, lobbying state and local officials, recruiting campaign volunteers, voter registration activities, get-out-the-vote activities, phone banks to influence elections or governmental activities, and administrative costs and tasks for all of these activities appear to be directly related to political, legislative or governmental purposes. For the Democrats United committee, whose mission is to support and oppose area candidates, the Event served as “a political event.” In addition to the purpose of recognizing past efforts of a staff member and campaign activist, the Event was a gathering of those Party officials, donors and activists who are important to campaign activities in the area. The expenditures are permissible so long as the gift to each attendee has a total cumulative value of less than $250 in a calendar year. (Section 89513(f)(3).)

Please note that individual attendees may be subject to disclosure and gift limit ($460) requirements under the Act for the receipt of a gift from either committee. (Section 87200, et al., and Section 89503.)
